claim: Herding and Terrier breed groups are statistically overrepresented for behavioral medicine referrals at Cornell (1997-2017), while Toy and Sporting groups are underrepresented, suggesting population-level breed-group associations with behavioral problems.
stance_target: weak/conditional validity of breed-group priors for behavioral risk
scope_conditions: Cornell University Hospital for Animals, 1997-2017; 822 behavioral cases vs. 51,052 general hospital cases; US-specific referral population; confounded by dog size, geography, owner motivation
quoted_support: The Herding (p=0.0124) and Terrier (p<0.0001) groups were over-represented in the behavioral medicine service as compared to all other services while Toy (p<0.0001) and Sporting (p<0.0001) groups were under-represented.

claim: 72.2% of dogs referred to veterinary behavioral medicine presented for aggression (overwhelmingly human-directed), consistent across multiple decades — aggression is the dominant behavioral problem requiring specialist referral.
stance_target: prevalence of aggression as behavioral problem in clinical context
scope_conditions: Cornell University Hospital, 1997-2017, n=1,923 cases; US referral population; selection bias toward larger/more dangerous dogs acknowledged
quoted_support: Over twenty years, 72.2% of dogs presented for aggression, 20.1% for anxieties/fears/phobias, and 7.4% for miscellaneous behavioral problems.

claim: Breed overrepresentation in behavioral referrals is heavily confounded by dog size and perceived bite risk — owners are more motivated to seek specialist help for large aggressive dogs than small ones, distorting breed statistics.
stance_target: breed-specific behavioral referral statistics as unbiased signals
scope_conditions: Cornell University behavioral clinic; expert author commentary on confounders
quoted_support: One could theorize that genetic predisposition might play a role in the incidence of behavioral problems. However, while there is certainly a genetic contribution to behavior and specific breeds might be over-represented for behavioral phenotypes, it is important to avoid placing too much emphasis on breed as a sole risk factor.

claim: Shelter- and rescue-acquired dogs significantly increased as a proportion of behavioral clinic referrals from 1997 to 2017, while breeder-acquired dogs decreased, indicating the rescue population is a growing behavioral medicine concern.
stance_target: behavioral risk profile of shelter/rescue dogs vs. breeder dogs
scope_conditions: Cornell University Hospital, n=1,923 over 20 years; source information available for 1,457 dogs; p<0.0001 for both trends
quoted_support: Dogs acquired from breeders decreased and dogs from shelters, rescues, or adopted as a stray increased over twenty years (p<0.0001).
